title: Storage Path — Cluster Failover or Multipath Events

value: Surfaces storage or hypervisor failover events evidencing infrastructure resilience monitoring.
grandmaExplanation: Surfaces storage or hypervisor failover events evidencing infrastructure resilience monitoring — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Normalize messages per vendor; (2) alert spikes; (3) tie to capacity planning.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: VMware or SAN logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `vmware:inv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Normalize messages per vendor; (2) alert spikes; (3) tie to capacity planning.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=infra sourcetype=vmware:inv earliest=-7d failover OR "path down"
| stats count by host, Message
```

Understanding this SPL

**Storage Path — Cluster Failover or Multipath Events (PR.IR-02)** — Surfaces storage or hypervisor failover events evidencing infrastructure resilience monitoring.

Documented **Data sources**: `vmware:inv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): VMware or SAN logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: infra; **sourcetype**: vmware:inv. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=infra, sourcetype=vmware:inv, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, Message** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Bar chart
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
